annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia?

he nonproductive cough, dyspnea, and fever, the widened arterialalveolar gradient, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by  *P. carinii.*
I shall not consider seriously the possibility of sarcoidosis in this patient, although the association of HIV infection and sarcoidosis has been reported, as expected.
I come to the possibility of mycobacterial disease in this patient.
I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patient

I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.

The ill-defined appearance of the granulomas is characteristic of granulomatous inflammation in patients with AIDS and differs from the well-formed compact granulomas of tuberculoid type seen in response to infections in patients who are not immunosuppressed.

If I am correct and this is a case of histoplasmosis, however, it is possible that the two episodes were related since the latency period between the onset of infection and the time of the diagnosis can be as long as six to eight months in this disease.

We believed that pneumocystis infection was possible because the serum LDH level was greater than 450 IU per liter and because the aerosolized pentamidine that he received would have decreased the yield of induction of sputum and bronchoscopic examination for that organism.

Therefore, the diagnosis can be difficult and should always be suspected, in particular in a patient from an ethnic group known to have a high prevalence of tuberculosis, which is the setting in the patient under discussion.
The absence of skin lesions and of the typical cherry-red, raised lesions in the tracheobronchial tree, however, would be unusual for a case of Kaposi's sarcoma.

the lack of positive smears on multiple specimens, the absence of obvious organ involvement outside the lungs, and the relatively slow evolution of the diffuse pulmonary infiltrates make a diagnosis of miliary tuberculosis less likely.
the radiographic pattern shown in the films of the chest, consisting mainly of diffuse interstitial and then reticulonodular infiltrates with possible hilar lymphadenopathy.

The differential diagnosis that we considered was strongly influenced by the miliary pattern on the x-ray films of the chest and included  *M. tuberculosis* infection,  *P. carinii* pneumonia, and histoplasmosi

Although histoplasmosis is often disseminated at the time of presentation and symptoms can be severe, mimicking a gram-negative septicemia, subacute illness with a high fever, substantial weight loss, and minor respiratory tract symptoms is not uncommon.
